frameworks.name: efficient coding
frameworks.one_line: neural sensitivity allocated to features more common in the environment

frameworks.name: gradient descent learning in neural networks
frameworks.one_line: gradient-based learning shown to naturally produce efficient-coding-like sensitivity

frameworks.name: artificial neural networks (supervised and unsupervised)
frameworks.one_line: object-recognition ANNs used as a model system for sensitivity patterns

core_question: Do efficient codes naturally emerge from gradient descent learning in neural networks without explicit resource constraints?
key_assumptions: sensitivity to stimulus features can be compared between biological and artificial systems
feature statistics in natural images carry the relevant prior
coding resources need not be explicitly constrained for efficient codes to arise
manipulations: feature frequency in input statistics (e.g., cardinal vs oblique orientations)
key_findings: ANNs trained for object recognition show sensitivity matching image feature statistics
gradient descent provably biases representations toward sensitivity to common features
the effect holds even without explicit coding-capacity constraints
the effect appears under both supervised and unsupervised training
interpretations: efficient codes can emerge as a natural by-product of gradient-like learning
human-like sensitivity asymmetries (e.g., oblique effect) may not require an explicit efficiency objective
